part-time assistant named Father Milt Glore.<|13.78|> <|13.92|> Father Milt had retired from the parish up the road in Muscle Shoals.<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and came to give us a few hours of work each week.<|5.20|> <|5.36|> Father Milt was in his 70s.<|7.54|> <|7.60|> I was about 30 years old, so I can't speak for him,<|11.60|> <|11.68|> but I'm certain I needed him more, or at least as much as he needed me.<|16.18|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.66|> Father Milt's great love in this parish was pastoral visitation.<|4.20|> <|4.48|> What he spent the bulk of his time doing each week<|7.02|> <|7.02|> was visiting people in nursing homes, assisted livings, or hospitals.<|11.48|> <|12.42|> That was the main reason why he worked with us.<|14.86|> <|14.98|> He loved being with people.<|16.92|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Preaching was actually not his love, although he had done a lot of it over several decades of ministry.<|8.14|> <|8.86|> Father Milt was a preacher who preached from a manuscript.<|11.44|> <|11.44|> He had a file full of old sermons.<|17.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He would sometimes create new ones and sometimes not.<|4.58|> <|4.68|> And I vividly remember once on Good Friday, he preached.<|8.36|> <|8.68|> And as I sat there near the altar, I listened and I thought, I think I've heard this one before.<|13.40|> <|13.96|> And indeed, I had.<|15.38|> <|15.46|> He had preached the exact same sermon the year before on Good Friday.<|19.14|> <|19.14|> Good Friday.<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When I was at the door after the service, someone said, a parishioner with a great sense of humor,<|4.50|> <|5.46|> I think he preached that one last year.<|7.90|> <|8.64|> And I said, indeed, he did.<|10.46|> <|10.98|> Father Milt was concerned we weren't listening.<|12.68|> <|17.18|> John's gospel is a lot like Father Milt.<|22.58|> <|22.80|> John, as a preacher, repeats himself,<|26.66|> <|26.66|> and he's a preacher.<|27.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.62|> This gospel reading, John 15, we get the words,<|4.88|> <|5.20|> abide or remain in Christ, abide in me,<|10.18|> <|10.26|> and the wonderful voluntary that our organist played for us<|13.60|> <|13.60|> picks up on just that theme.<|15.46|> <|15.86|> John loves this word.<|17.06|> <|17.16|> He loves it so much that he repeats it over and over again.<|20.44|> <|20.44|> Forty times in John's gospel, the word abide or remain is used by him.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In Matthew, Mark, and Luke combined, they use this word 12 times, but 40 in John's.<|8.26|> <|8.80|> One scholar defines this word abide or remain as deep attachment.<|16.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> deep attachment, and the only thing I would add to that is deep attachment over the long haul.<|7.24|> <|7.50|> Implicit in this verb is abiding in Christ for a long time, even for eternity.<|14.18|> <|15.22|> John loves this word because it's the theme of so many of his stories.<|19.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Please.<|1.46|><|endoftext|> can actually get better.<|13.02|> <|13.94|> Or later in John's Gospel, shortest verse in the Bible,<|19.14|> <|19.30|> Jesus wept.<|20.20|> <|21.12|> Jesus wept when his friend Lazarus dies.<|23.60|> <|23.60|> And it's an image of Jesus abiding or remaining with us,<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> in our grief,<|1.78|> <|3.20|> in our very ordinary emotions<|5.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> saying mysteriously and out of the darkness to Mary Magdalene,<|4.54|> <|4.62|> just whispering her name.<|6.26|> <|6.58|> And that becomes the word, that becomes the moment of intimacy<|9.90|> <|9.90|> that allows her to recognize him,<|12.32|> <|12.76|> speaking as he is from the other side of death.<|16.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And it's a symbol, it's an image, it's a story that's about God in Christ holding us dear forever,<|8.50|> <|9.02|> abiding with us in eternity, that all that we are and all that we hold dear will not be lost.<|17.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I<|1.76|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.52|> It also relates to this agricultural image of the vine and the branches.<|8.82|> <|10.64|> The vine and the branches, a common image from Jesus' day and age.<|15.24|> <|16.42|> And I find it so comforting that in this image, implicit in this image, is a promise<|22.40|> <|22.40|> that Christ will grow with us, will stay with us,<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> no matter what happens, that over time Christ will grow with us as we face and feel the changes<|8.28|> <|8.28|> and chances of this life. It's not just a promise for the present moment, but for all of time.<|15.78|> <|17.00|> And the vine produces wine or joy.<|21.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.66|> I'm sure that St. John's has heard the sermon before that joy is not the same thing as happiness.<|9.02|> <|9.96|> I've heard that sermon repeated many times myself.<|12.96|> <|14.32|> I will not repeat it in part because I don't know that I actually agree with it.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Joy is certainly always more than happiness, but perhaps it's never less than happiness,<|10.12|> <|10.30|> but we're splitting hairs.<|11.62|> <|13.34|> I do believe that what this gospel reading is offering us, though, that relates to joy<|18.62|> <|18.62|> is that there is deep joy in the world.<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and maybe even peace of mind<|3.08|> <|3.08|> in coming to trust that you were loved infinitely in Christ,<|8.56|> <|9.78|> in coming to trust that you always abide in Christ,<|13.92|> <|13.96|> no matter what you face and feel.<|15.76|> <|16.44|> And you do that not because of your faith,<|18.88|> <|18.88|> not because of your prayers,<|22.58|> <|22.90|> and least of all because you attempt to be religious,<|27.04|> <|27.54|> but,<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that we're able to abide in Christ because Christ first abides in us.<|6.48|> <|8.18|> In other words, the heavy lifting of the vine and the branches has been done by Almighty God.<|16.72|> <|17.90|> As Jesus says,<|18.94|> <|19.80|> "'My Father is the vine grower.'"<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.84|> We're able to abide in Christ because Christ is already in us.<|5.62|> <|5.78|> Christ already relates to all that we are.<|8.64|> <|8.64|> Christ already is one with us in mind, body, and soul,<|14.24|> <|14.40|> and maybe even in what we feel, our emotional life.<|18.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.10|> The great 20th century Cistercian Thomas Merton<|6.00|> <|6.00|> picked up on this very theme in a great and little book<|10.50|> <|10.50|> called Thoughts on Solitude.<|14.04|> <|15.44|> And it's a book that's written for all of us,<|18.14|> <|18.20|> not just for religious, but for people who think about<|20.88|> <|20.88|> the moments in our lives in which we face solitude,<|25.26|> <|25.26|> whether that solitude is chosen or not.<|28.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But in this particular moment that relates so much to this passage, Merton says that the soul of Jesus is united to the word of God.<|12.20|> <|13.12|> And at the same time enjoys and without conflict enjoys the most simple and common and intimate of human emotions.<|23.42|> <|26.62|> Affection.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> happiness, grief, wonder, anxiety, peace.<|10.58|> <|12.42|> Jesus enjoys all, relates to all of these human emotions.<|18.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that's why we can abide in Christ over the long haul.<|3.96|> <|4.90|> Not because of our faith or hard work or even our prayers.<|10.40|> <|11.68|> Because what we face and feel among the changes and chances of this life,<|16.20|> <|17.02|> Christ relates to all of it.<|19.06|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What?<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.10|> So John is right.<|2.66|> <|3.58|> Some things bear repeating.<|5.32|> <|6.70|> Abide in Christ as Christ abides in you.<|11.10|>
